statusLine: Americas oil markets face supply drawdowns and elevated prices amid Middle East shipping disruptions, with WTI climbing above $101 as US crude stocks fall by 5.2 million barrels.
fullAnalysis:
US petroleum markets are showing signs of tightening supply conditions, with commercial crude inventories falling by 5.2 million barrels to 392.7 million barrels in the week ending May 12. This drawdown, combined with declines in gasoline (-2.5 million barrels) and distillate (-1.3 million barrels) stocks, has contributed to upward pressure on domestic fuel prices. US production remains robust at 13.573 million barrels per day, but the inventory declines suggest demand is outpacing supply additions.

WTI crude gained 3.55% in the week to May 12, reaching $101.03 per barrel, while maintaining its discount to Brent at $5.79. The price rally reflects both domestic inventory tightness and global supply chain risks. US retail gasoline prices have surged to $4.581 per gallon (+32.4 cents), with diesel at $5.64 per gallon (+28.9 cents), indicating the inventory drawdowns are translating directly to consumer costs.

Critical maritime advisories highlight severe disruptions to global oil flows, particularly through the Red Sea and Persian Gulf chokepoints. Houthi attacks in the Red Sea and Bab el Mandeb Strait, combined with Iranian threats in the Strait of Hormuz, are forcing tankers onto longer routes and driving up shipping costs. While the Panama Canal remains operational for Americas-focused flows, the Middle East disruptions are affecting global crude pricing and availability of alternative supplies.

The combination of domestic inventory drawdowns, elevated production levels, and international shipping risks suggests Americas oil markets are entering a more precarious balance. With WTI above $100 and retail fuel prices climbing sharply, the region faces potential supply security challenges if current trends continue or worsen.
keyPoints: US crude inventories fell 5.2 million barrels to 392.7 million barrels, with gasoline and distillate stocks also declining significantly
WTI crude gained 3.55% week-on-week to $101.03/bbl, trading at a $5.79 discount to Brent
US retail gasoline prices jumped 32.4 cents to $4.581/gallon, with diesel up 28.9 cents to $5.64/gallon
Critical maritime advisories highlight ongoing Houthi attacks in Red Sea shipping lanes and Iranian threats in Persian Gulf
US oil production remains strong at 13.573 million barrels per day, but demand appears to be outpacing supply additions
Strategic Petroleum Reserve stands at 77.6 million barrels as commercial inventories tighten
